summary: When I arrived in the United States, I expected to find the same kind of safe and playful environment I had in Honduras. However, I quickly realized that things were very different here, especially in my neighborhood where people carried guns and it wasn’t safe to play outside. My mom warned me not to go out because of the danger, and after witnessing a violent incident, I stopped playing outdoors altogether. I also noticed that celebrations like Independence Day were much more subdued here compared to Honduras, which made me feel a bit disconnected from the community.

Growing up in Honduras was very different from my life in the United States. Back in Honduras, we could play outside freely until late at night without any fear or danger, and the community was lively and safe. I remember the Independence Day celebrations there were full of parades, music, dancing, and decorated cars, which brought everyone together in joy. Leaving Honduras was very hard for me and my family, especially my grandma who cried when I left, saying I was the most precious thing she had. Honduras will always hold a special place in my heart because of those innocent and happy times.

In the United States, I noticed that Independence Day is not celebrated with much enthusiasm in my community. Unlike in Honduras, where Independence Day is marked by vibrant parades with drums, music, dancing, and decorated cars, here people mostly stay indoors and keep things quiet. This difference made me feel like the spirit of celebration and togetherness was missing here. It was one of the things that made me realize how much I missed the lively cultural traditions of my home country.
